Persistence

Persistence is the ability to maintain long-term access to a compromised system across reboots, credential changes, or other interruptions by leveraging legitimate system mechanisms.

Common Persistence Techniques

  • Account Manipulation - Modify accounts, credentials, or permissions.
  • Autostart Execution - Run code at boot or user logon.
  • Services & Daemons - Create or modify services for automatic execution.
  • Scheduled Tasks / Cron Jobs - Execute payloads on a schedule.
  • Execution Hijacking - Abuse DLL loading, COM, or PATH mechanisms.
  • Server Components - Deploy web shells or malicious server modules.
  • Application Extensions - Office add-ins, browser extensions, plugins.
  • Authentication Components - Modify authentication mechanisms.
  • Valid Accounts - Reuse legitimate local, domain, or cloud accounts.

Key Points

  • Goal: Maintain access after reboot, logoff, or password changes.
  • Method: Abuse trusted OS or application features instead of repeatedly exploiting vulnerabilities.
  • Often Combined With: Privilege Escalation and Defense Evasion.
  • Detection: Monitor new services, startup items, scheduled tasks, account changes, and authentication modifications.

Resources

MITRE ATT&CK TA0003
